misconceptionSummary: Students miss factor pairs by not working systematically, often omitting 1 and the number itself.

visuals.ruleText: Find factors by testing divisors from 1 upward — list pairs
visuals.subText: Stop when the divisor reaches or crosses the square root of the number

steps.narration: Factors come in pairs. For any factor smaller than the square root, there is a corresponding larger factor. Work systematically from one upward, checking each potential divisor and stopping when the pairs meet in the middle.

Test each number starting from one. One times twenty-four, two times twelve, three times eight, four times six — all exact divisions. Testing five gives a decimal, so five is not a factor. The square root of twenty-four is about four point nine, so we stop after four.
From the pairs, list both members in order: one, two, three, four, six, eight, twelve, twenty-four. Twenty-four has eight factors. Always include one and the number itself.

visuals.label: Work in pairs from 1 upward — stop at the square root

steps.narration: One, two, three, four, six, eight, twelve, twenty-four. Systematic pair-testing ensures no factors are missed.

misconceptionStrip.text: Common mistake: listing only some factors and missing others — especially 1 and the number itself. Work in pairs from 1 upward and stop at the square root to guarantee a complete list.
misconceptionStrip.audioNarration: Work in factor pairs from 1 upward. Every factor below the square root has a matching factor above it. Stop when the pairs meet — and always include 1 and the number itself.
